meta.channelTheme: Lifetime journey — solo adventure rider crossing North America on a Suzuki DR650
meta.tone: Personal, raw, authentic. Equal parts travel film and honest riding diary.

days.videoTitle: I Crossed Into the USA on a Motorcycle — Canada Border to Finger Lakes | Day 1
days.hook: Passport out, engine running, border officer staring at the bike. This is the moment you stop planning and start riding — across an international border, alone, into America.
days.storyArc: Start the episode at home — Toronto morning routine, gear-up, roll out. Build tension through the border crossing (the questions, the wait, the wave-through). Release into the open road of upstate New York. End on an emotional note at the host's front door: Day 1, done.
shots: Time-lapse leaving Toronto at dawn — CN Tower in background
GoPro forward-facing on the 401 / QEW run to the border
Passport check moment — wide shot showing the booths
First American road sign after crossing — react on camera
I-90 entry ramp in New York — open tarmac stretching east
Finger Lakes panoramic — lake between tree-covered hills
Arrival at host's — helmet off, handshake, relief on face
facts: The Lewiston-Queenston Bridge (Niagara crossing) was built in 1962 — its deck is 305 feet above the Niagara River.
The Finger Lakes were carved by glaciers 10,000 years ago — legend says they are the handprints of the Great Spirit pressing onto the earth.
Seneca Lake is 618 feet deep — deeper than Lake Ontario — and never fully freezes.
McGraw, NY sits in Cortland County, once the geographic center of New York's upstate dairy farming belt.
Canadians make 36+ million personal trips to the USA every year — but few on a loaded ADV bike planning 35 days.
days.culture: The US-Canada border is the longest undefended border in the world (5,525 miles) — yet crossing on a motorcycle with gear feels distinctly different from a car. American CBP officers often react with curiosity to solo moto travelers: be honest, be brief, keep your documents in an outer pocket.
talkingPoints: Why you chose the DR650 for this kind of trip
What it feels like emotionally to leave home for 35 days
The difference in road 'feel' the moment you cross into the US
Finger Lakes as an overlooked gem — wine country, deep glacial lakes, quiet roads
days.cta: Comment: What's one thing you'd pack for a 35-day US motorcycle loop? I'll reply to every comment on Day 1.

days.videoTitle: Riding Into New York — Finger Lakes to Long Island on a DR650 | Day 2
days.hook: Most riders avoid New York City entirely. I rode straight toward it.
days.storyArc: Open with the beauty of upstate NY — quiet Finger Lakes roads, morning fog on the water. Contrast it with the growing density as you push toward the metro. Capture the Thruway toll plazas (cashless — talk about EZPass for Canadian plates). Deliver the 'city vs. open road' tension. Land in Southampton — old money, beach town, motorcycles attract stares.
shots: Morning mist on one of the Finger Lakes — wide and slow
NY Thruway open stretch — lanes wide, fields either side
First 'Welcome to Long Island' or NYC-approaching sign
George Washington Bridge if you route over it — iron arch over the Hudson
Southampton streets — manicured hedges, quiet mid-week
Bike parked against East Coast beach backdrop
facts: The New York State Thruway (I-90) is 570 miles long — the longest toll superhighway in the United States.
Long Island is home to 7.8 million people — if it were a state it would be larger than 38 US states by population.
Southampton was established in 1640 — one of the oldest English-speaking settlements in North America.
The Hamptons zip codes (like 11968) routinely appear on lists of the highest median household incomes in the entire country.
Hip-hop culture has deep roots in Long Island — Public Enemy, Rakim, and Eric B. all came from here.
days.culture: Long Island is two worlds: the working-class west end near Queens, and the ultra-wealthy East End (the Hamptons). Arriving on a loaded adventure bike in Southampton turns heads — the locals there are used to Ferraris, not DR650s. That contrast is your story.
talkingPoints: Navigating toll roads on Canadian plates without EZPass
The social geography of Long Island — diversity from west to east
Why a motorcycle rider feels out of place in the Hamptons — and why that's interesting
Setting up the next big day: NYC → DC → Shenandoah
days.cta: Like this video if you've ever ridden somewhere you felt completely out of place — and loved it.

days.videoTitle: New York → Washington DC → Shenandoah Valley in One Day | Day 3
days.hook: Three different worlds in one riding day: the biggest city in America, the capital of the free world, and one of the oldest mountain ranges on Earth.
days.storyArc: Open with the chaos and energy of leaving NYC / Long Island. Build across the NJ/MD corridor — I-95 reality check. Arrive in DC: slow roll past monuments, Capitol, White House. The emotional pivot: leaving the power-center and climbing toward Luray — mountains rising in the mirror. End at Shenandoah with a sense of 'this is what America really looks like.'
shots: NYC skyline or bridge exit — wide, cinematic
GoPro through NJ Turnpike and Delaware Memorial Bridge
Washington Monument and Lincoln Memorial — ride-by at low speed
Capitol dome close-up — iconic American shot
Shenandoah Valley vista opening up as you descend from the ridge
Luray at dusk — small town main street
facts: Washington DC is not a state — it is a federal district, so its 700,000 residents have no voting representation in Congress (only a non-voting delegate).
I-95 is the most-traveled highway in America, running 1,908 miles from Maine to Florida.
The Shenandoah Valley is part of the Great Appalachian Valley — a continuous lowland corridor that runs for 1,200 miles from Alabama to New York.
Luray Caverns contains the world's only working stalagmite organ — the 'Stalacpipe Organ' covers 3.5 acres and is the world's largest natural musical instrument.
The Appalachian Mountains are among the oldest on Earth — estimated at 480 million years old, older than trees.
days.culture: Riding through DC on a motorcycle is unlike anything else in America. The city was designed by French architect Pierre Charles L'Enfant in 1791 specifically to project power. Wide ceremonial boulevards, sight-lines to monuments, symbolic geometry — riding through it on a loaded bike makes all of that feel close and real.
talkingPoints: The weight of history in Washington DC — how it feels to ride past those monuments
I-95 corridor congestion reality — and how motorcycles deal with it
Luray Caverns as an underrated world-class wonder
The Shenandoah as the spine of eastern American history: Civil War battlefields within miles of the highway
days.cta: Drop a flag emoji for every US state you've visited. Let's see who's got the most.

days.videoTitle: Riding the Blue Ridge Corridor — Virginia's Best Hidden Roads | Day 4
days.hook: Virginia has roads that rival anything in the Alps. Most people drive past them on the Interstate.
days.storyArc: The morning departure from Luray sets up this as a 'discovery day' — no mega landmarks, just the pure experience of riding southwest Virginia. Introduce the Blue Ridge Parkway concept. Find Natural Bridge. Deliver a personal reflection on what riding through American history actually means.
shots: Blue Ridge Parkway entrance sign — classic wooden NPS marker
Long sweeping BRP road ahead — no cars, autumn or summer light
Natural Bridge from below looking up — 215 feet tall
Appalachian Trail crossing sign on the roadway
Small-town Virginia main street — gas station, diner, locals
Sunset over the Shenandoah ridge from above
facts: The Blue Ridge Parkway is 469 miles long and has never allowed commercial trucks — ever. No billboards either.
Natural Bridge was owned by Thomas Jefferson, who paid 20 shillings for it in 1774 and called it 'the most sublime of Nature's works.'
The Appalachian Trail, which crosses this route, is 2,190 miles long — hikers completing it are called 'Thru-hikers' and typically take 5-7 months.
Pulaski, Virginia is named after Polish-born General Casimir Pulaski, who died at the 1779 Battle of Savannah fighting for American independence.
Southwest Virginia sits in the heart of Coalfield Appalachia — a region with one of the most complex economic and cultural histories in America.
days.culture: The Appalachian culture of southwest Virginia is distinct from the rest of the South and the rest of the Northeast — it has its own music (old-time, not country), its own food, its own way of dealing with strangers. Riders are usually welcomed with curiosity. This is the heartland of American folk culture.
talkingPoints: Why the Blue Ridge Parkway is one of the greatest engineering achievements in American history — and it's free to ride
The story of Natural Bridge: Jefferson, early America, colonial tourism
Appalachian identity — not a stereotype, a real living culture
Setting up the big emotional moment: riding into Tennessee tomorrow
days.cta: Have you ever been to Appalachia? Tell me what surprised you most.

days.videoTitle: The Most Dangerous Beautiful Road in America — Tail of the Dragon | Day 5
days.hook: 318 curves in 11 miles. No passing zones. No driveways. No guardrails. Welcome to the Dragon.
days.storyArc: Build from the departure in Pulaski toward the Smokies — the landscape changing, getting wilder. Arrive at Deals Gap. Capture the Tail of the Dragon tree of shame (bikes that crashed). Execute the pass — show skill, show respect. Land in Sevierville and pull the Dolly Parton surprise fact. End with the Smokies glowing at sunset.
shots: Tail of the Dragon signage — 'Tree of Shame' with crashed bike parts
GoPro on the Dragon: corner after corner after corner
Pullout somewhere mid-Dragon: scan the mountain below
Deals Gap fueling / parking area atmosphere
Smoky Mountains first panoramic: the 'smoke' visible
Sevierville: Dolly Parton's statue in front of the courthouse
facts: Tail of the Dragon (US-129) has 318 curves in 11 miles — no driveways, no intersections, no escape — it was designed that way.
The Great Smoky Mountains are named for the natural 'smoke' — actually terpenes (organic compounds) evaporating from the trees, creating a visible blue-grey haze.
The Smokies are the most visited national park in America with over 12 million visitors per year — more than the Grand Canyon and Yellowstone combined.
Sevierville, TN is the birthplace of Dolly Parton. Her bronze statue stands in front of the Sevier County Courthouse.
The Appalachian Mountains here are among the most biodiverse temperate forests on Earth — more tree species than all of northern Europe.
days.culture: The Dragon draws riders from across North America like a pilgrimage site. There's a whole subculture: photographers at 'Dragon's Tail' marking your run, the Tree of Shame (hung with parts from crashes), the Deals Gap store that's been serving riders for decades. Respect the road — the 'enthusiastic' locals on sport bikes know every corner and will pass you on a blind curve.
talkingPoints: Technique for technical mountain passes on a loaded adventure bike — slow in, accelerate out
The Tree of Shame and riding culture ego management
Dolly Parton as a symbol of Appalachian identity and pride
GSMNP vehicle reservations — the park has timed entry in peak season
days.cta: Would you ride the Dragon? YES or NO in the comments — explain your answer.

days.videoTitle: Riding Through the Soul of America — Smokies to Nashville to Memphis | Day 6
days.hook: One day. Three music capitals. The road that wrote American music.
days.storyArc: Leave the mountains, ride west through rolling Tennessee. Nashville: quick fuel stop but soak up Music Row energy. Acknowledge the civil rights history — this city changed America. Arrive in Memphis near dark. Beale Street after dinner — the sound of America coming from every door.
shots: Exit the Smokies — mountain in the rear camera, flat country ahead
Nashville skyline from the highway — the Batman Building (AT&T tower)
Broadway Street Nashville if time allows — neon signs, honky-tonks
Tennessee River crossing — wide water, industrial port
Memphis: Beale Street at dusk — lights, music, movement
Mississippi River from the Hernando de Soto Bridge bike path
facts: Nashville has more than 150 live music venues — more per capita than anywhere else in the world.

The 1960 Nashville sit-ins, organized by students from Fisk University, were among the most disciplined and successful of the entire Civil Rights Movement — 81 days after they started, Nashville became the first major Southern city to desegregate lunch counters.

Memphis, Tennessee sits on the Chickasaw Bluffs above the Mississippi River. The name 'Memphis' was deliberately chosen by the city's founders because ancient Memphis, Egypt, was also located on a great river.
Beale Street has been a hub of African-American culture and music since the 1860s — W.C. Handy, 'Father of the Blues,' published the first Blues compositions here in 1912.
B.B. King, Elvis Presley, Johnny Cash, Roy Orbison, and Jerry Lee Lewis all got their starts in Memphis within a single decade — the 1950s.
days.culture: Memphis and Nashville represent two very different Tennessees. Nashville is commercially polished, aspirational, bachelorette-party country. Memphis is rawer, deeply soulful, historically significant, and underappreciated. The music that came out of Memphis (Blues, early Rock, Soul) directly created most of the global popular music that followed. Ride through with that in mind.
talkingPoints: Why Memphis doesn't get the recognition it deserves — the birthplace of rock and roll
The Nashville vs. Memphis tension — commercial success vs. soul
Tennessee River and Mississippi River — the hydraulic spine of the American interior
Sun Studio in Memphis: $13 self-guided tour, worth every penny for content
days.cta: Team Nashville or Team Memphis? Drop it in the comments and tell me why.

days.videoTitle: Crossing the Mississippi River — Into the Deep South Heartland | Day 7
days.hook: The Mississippi River. Every American story crosses it eventually.
days.storyArc: Memphis bridge crossing is cinematic — the river at highway speed, industrial scale, historical weight. Cross into Arkansas. Flat, wide open, sky dominating the frame. Stop in Little Rock — talk about Central High. Ride through late afternoon Arkansas toward Jacksonville. End the episode on the quiet flat roads of the mid-South.
shots: Hernando de Soto Bridge crossing — wide Mississippi, bridge steel
First 'Welcome to Arkansas' sign
Little Rock city view — the Statehouse, Central High School exterior
Arkansas flatlands — straight road to the horizon, sky massive
Jacksonville sunset — suburban quiet, bike on the side road
facts: The Mississippi River is 2,340 miles long and drains 41% of the continental United States — 31 states and 2 Canadian provinces drain into it.

In 1957, nine Black students (the 'Little Rock Nine') attempted to enroll at Central High School in Little Rock, Arkansas — the confrontation required President Eisenhower to send the 101st Airborne Division to enforce desegregation.
Arkansas is the only US state that commercially mines diamonds — Crater of Diamonds State Park is the world's only diamond mine where the public can search and keep what they find.
Hot Springs, Arkansas was so neutral ground during Prohibition that Al Capone and other gangsters vacationed there openly — it was called 'the city that crime built.'
The Arkansas River, which Little Rock straddles, is navigable for 448 miles and connects to the Mississippi via the McClellan-Kerr waterway.
days.culture: Arkansas is deeply underrated by the coastal travel media. It has some of the most dramatic Ozark landscape, legitimate American history (Little Rock Nine is as important as any event in 20th-century America), and a river culture tied directly to the continent's geography. Riding through it with knowledge makes every mile more meaningful.
talkingPoints: The Little Rock Nine — what really happened and why it matters
The Mississippi River crossing as a psychological threshold — 'the West begins here'
Arkansas diamonds — you can literally find a diamond there
Hot Springs: where organized crime met American health tourism in the 1930s
days.cta: What do you know about Arkansas? I bet it surprised you. Let me know below.

days.videoTitle: Riding Into the Heart of Texas — Fort Worth Home Stop | Day 8
days.hook: After 2,000+ km from Toronto, the bike rolls into a garage and stops. It's not the end — it's the service bay.
days.storyArc: This is the 'home base' episode. Lean into the ritual of the maintenance stop — oil check, tire pressure, chain, what's rattling. Fort Worth Stockyards as B-roll. The emotional weight of having a home on the road. Rest, repack, then the west begins tomorrow.
shots: Dallas/Fort Worth skyline approach — sprawling and flat
Fort Worth Stockyards — cattle drive at 11:30am or 4pm (daily)
Longhorn cattle in the street — the most Texas thing ever
Bike in a real garage — tools out, working
Fort Worth Sundance Square at night if staying in
Repacking: organized chaos of gear laid out on the floor
facts: Texas is the only state that was an independent republic before joining the United States — the Republic of Texas existed from 1836 to 1846.
The Fort Worth Stockyards hosted a daily cattle drive every day since 1866 — a living piece of the Chisholm Trail era. It still happens twice a day.
Texas has the highest speed limit in the US — 85 mph (137 km/h) on Texas State Highway 130.
The Texas longhorn steer can have a horn span of over 7 feet. The Fort Worth Stockyards herd is one of the few fully authentic longhorn herds remaining.
Fort Worth and Dallas are two completely different cities despite being 30 miles apart — Dallas is finance and fashion, Fort Worth is 'where the West begins' (literally its official motto).
days.culture: Fort Worth has a genuine cowboy culture that Dallas lacks. The Stockyards is not a theme park — real cattle auctions, real working cowboys, and the sense that the Old West is still alive here. For a Canadian riding through, this contrast with the East Coast days is jarring in the best possible way.
talkingPoints: What a home base stop teaches you — what you'd change if you started over
The myth of Texas vs the reality
Fort Worth Stockyards as one of the only places in the US where the 19th century is still a daily routine
The west starts here: tomorrow is Route 66, the desert, and the unknown
days.cta: If you could have a home base anywhere in the USA, where would it be? I'll pick the most interesting answer and make it a stop.

days.videoTitle: Route 66 — Riding America's Most Mythic Road to the Midpoint | Day 9
days.hook: There are roads, and then there is Route 66 — the road that invented road trips.
days.storyArc: Fort Worth north to Amarillo then west on 66. The landscape expanding, the sky impossibly large. Cadillac Ranch — obligatory, absurd, perfect content. Adrian, TX: the literal geographic midpoint of Route 66. The emotional weight of standing exactly halfway between Chicago and Los Angeles on the Mother Road.
shots: Route 66 shield on the road — iconic symbol
Texas panhandle: road ruler-straight to the vanishing point
Cadillac Ranch approach — ten Cadillacs buried nose-first, spray-painted
Spray-painting your country's flag or name on a Cadillac (bring a can)
Adrian, TX midpoint sign: '1139 miles to Chicago · 1139 miles to Los Angeles'
Midpoint Cafe exterior — '50s diner, unchanged since the highway died
facts: Route 66 was commissioned in 1926 and decommissioned in 1985 — replaced by the Interstate system. John Steinbeck called it 'The Mother Road' in The Grapes of Wrath.

Cadillac Ranch was created in 1974 by Amarillo billionaire Stanley Marsh 3 and a San Francisco art group. The Cadillacs are from model years 1948–1964. Anyone is allowed — even encouraged — to spray-paint them.
Adrian, Texas is the exact geographic midpoint of Route 66 — 1,139 miles from Chicago's starting point and 1,139 miles from the Santa Monica Pier in Los Angeles.
Amarillo means 'yellow' in Spanish — named by early settlers for the yellow wildflowers that covered the surrounding plains.
Palo Duro Canyon near Amarillo is 120 miles long and 800 feet deep — the second largest canyon in the United States, called 'the Grand Canyon of Texas.'
days.culture: Route 66 is America's most mythologized road — it carried Dust Bowl refugees west in the 1930s, returning soldiers in the 1940s, and free-spirited travelers in the 1960s. Driving it today is an act of historical tourism. The ghost towns and closed motels are not sad — they are honest monuments to every era that lived on this road.
talkingPoints: Why Route 66 matters beyond nostalgia: it's the story of American migration
The Grapes of Wrath — Steinbeck rode this road and made it immortal
Cadillac Ranch: one of the most accessible pieces of public art in America
Adrian's Midpoint Cafe — the 'ugly pie' is famous. Try it.
The windmills of west Texas — clean energy on the old Dust Bowl
days.cta: Have you driven any part of Route 66? Tell me where — I want to see your best photo in the comments.

days.videoTitle: Texas to New Mexico — Where America Gets Weird and Beautiful | Day 10
days.hook: New Mexico has an official state question. It's not 'what's your name?' It's: 'Red or green?'
days.storyArc: I-40 west is relentless and meditative. Cross into New Mexico — the light changes, the landscape changes, the culture changes. Albuquerque as a cultural crossroads: Native American, Spanish colonial, Anglo American, Breaking Bad. End the episode with green chile heat and the promise of mountains tomorrow.
shots: 'Welcome to New Mexico — Land of Enchantment' state line sign
High desert plateau — mesas, brush, impossible sky
Albuquerque Old Town plaza — Spanish colonial architecture
Petroglyph National Monument nearby if detour allows
A bowl of green chile posole or sopapilla — local food content
Los Alamos direction sign — subtle atomic age history
facts: New Mexico is the only US state with an official state question — 'Red or green?' refers to which chile sauce you want on your food. The official state answer is 'Christmas' (both).
Albuquerque was named after the Duke of Alburquerque, Viceroy of New Spain — settlers dropped one 'r' over the years and the spelling never recovered.
Breaking Bad was almost entirely filmed in Albuquerque — Walter White's house, Los Pollos Hermanos (actually Twisters restaurant), Saul Goodman's office — all still standing and still touristic.
New Mexico has more PhDs per capita than any other state — largely due to Sandia National Laboratories, Los Alamos National Laboratory, and three major universities.
Petroglyph National Monument on Albuquerque's west side contains more than 24,000 images carved into volcanic rock by ancestral Pueblo people over 700 years.
days.culture: New Mexico operates at three cultural layers simultaneously — you can go from a Pueblo feast day to a Spanish colonial plaza to a Breaking Bad film location in the same afternoon. The food is genuinely distinct (Hatch green chile has its own harvest season and devoted culture), the light is unlike anywhere else, and the state has a quiet, confident identity unlike the more commercially known Southwest states.
talkingPoints: New Mexico's food: why it's in a category of its own (not Mexican food, not Tex-Mex)
Breaking Bad filming locations as a form of dark tourism
The Trinity Site — where the first nuclear bomb was detonated in 1945, in southern New Mexico
Tomorrow: the Million Dollar Highway — one of the most dramatic roads on the continent
days.cta: Red or green? Tell me in the comments. If you've had real New Mexico green chile, you already know.

days.videoTitle: The Million Dollar Highway — Riding One of Earth's Most Spectacular Roads | Day 11
days.hook: No guardrails. 11,000 feet of elevation. Sheer cliff drops on one side. This road was built by people who did not value your life above their schedule.
days.storyArc: Climb from Albuquerque into the San Juans — the elevation rising, temperature dropping, landscape becoming alpine. The moment US-550 reveals itself: sudden exposure, cliff edges, vertical mountain walls. Silverton arrival: a Victorian mining town frozen in 1880. Camp at altitude. Stars at 9,000 feet.
shots: Albuquerque skyline in the rearview — desert city shrinking
US-550 switchback climbing — mountain wall on one side, air on the other
Ouray, CO from above — the 'Switzerland of America' in its valley
Million Dollar Highway exposed section: no guardrail, direct drop
Silverton main street: false-fronted Victorian buildings, mountain-ringed
Campsite at elevation: stars over the San Juans
facts: The 'Million Dollar Highway' name has three competing explanations: the road fill contained $1M worth of gold ore; it cost $1M per mile to build; or 'you couldn't pay me a million dollars to drive it again.'
The San Juan Mountains are a volcanic field — the remnant of a massive caldera complex. You are literally riding through the collapsed center of a supervolcano.

Silverton, Colorado has a permanent population of around 500 people and sits at 9,318 feet elevation — it is served by the Durango & Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad, the highest standard-gauge railroad endpoint in North America.

The Million Dollar Highway has no guardrails on several sections — Colorado DOT has said that guardrails would create a false sense of security without actually stopping a plunge. The drops are 2,000+ feet in places.
Ouray, Colorado is called the 'Switzerland of America' — it sits in a natural bowl surrounded by 14,000-foot peaks on three sides.
days.culture: The San Juan region has a boom-bust mining history (gold, silver, uranium) that left ghost towns, Victorian architecture, and a deeply stubborn independence in the people who stayed. Silverton is authentically that — not polished for tourism, just itself. Riders and overlanders are a major part of the local economy.
talkingPoints: How to actually ride an exposed mountain road on a loaded bike — technique and mindset
The supervolcano fact: you're riding through geological history
Altitude effects at 9,000 feet — body, bike performance, cold nights
Tomorrow: descending into the Four Corners desert — the biggest contrast on the trip
days.cta: Would you ride the Million Dollar Highway? Write YES/NO and what your biggest concern would be.

days.videoTitle: Four Corners Desert — The Land That Movies Made Sacred | Day 12
days.hook: Monument Valley isn't in any national park. It's Navajo land. And nothing looks like it anywhere on Earth.
days.storyArc: Descend from Silverton into the Four Corners desert. Cross the Colorado-Utah-New Mexico-Arizona convergence. US-491 — the former Devil's Highway. Mexican Hat rock formation. Monument Valley appears at a distance like a mirage. End at Mexican Hat campground beside the San Juan River: profound desert quiet.
shots: Four Corners Monument — stand in four states simultaneously
US-491 straight through desert — the road film directors love
Mexican Hat rock formation — the inverted sombrero rock
Monument Valley first view from distance: the three Mittens on the horizon
San Juan River at Mexican Hat — red water, red canyon walls
Sunset from campsite: the sky in the desert Southwest is unlike anywhere
facts: Monument Valley is not a national park — it is located on Navajo Nation land and managed as Navajo Tribal Park. Entry requires purchasing a tribal permit.

Director John Ford filmed nine Westerns in Monument Valley between 1939 and 1964, creating the visual vocabulary of the American West for the entire world — even people who have never been to America know what Monument Valley looks like.
US-491 was renumbered from US-666 in 2003 after years of complaints about the 'Devil's Highway' designation, and because the route signs were constantly stolen.
Mexican Hat, Utah has a permanent population of approximately 31 people. The town is named for a sandstone formation that resembles an inverted sombrero.
The Navajo Nation is the largest Native American reservation in the United States — at 27,425 square miles, it is larger than the state of West Virginia.
days.culture: The Navajo Nation is a sovereign nation within the United States — it has its own government, police force, and laws. When you are on Navajo land, you are a guest. Photography rules apply (always ask before photographing people), and some sacred areas are off-limits entirely. The people you meet here have a specific, complex, and often painful relationship with the American government — approach it with genuine curiosity and humility.
talkingPoints: Why Monument Valley looks 'familiar' even to people who've never been there — the power of cinema
Navajo Nation sovereignty: what it means in practice for a visitor
US-666 to US-491: why a number change took 20 years and required an act of Congress
The silence of the desert — content worth capturing: ambient sound, no narration
days.cta: What's the most remote place you've ever slept? I want to hear the stories below.

days.videoTitle: 500 km Epic — Horseshoe Bend, Antelope Canyon, and Bryce | Day 13
days.hook: This is the day the itinerary called 'EPIC.' 500 kilometers. Three world-class wonders. One sunset.
days.storyArc: Pre-dawn start from Mexican Hat. Ride to Page for Horseshoe Bend and Antelope Canyon — both require planning and energy. Long desert haul toward Kanab and up to Bryce. Sunset at the Bryce amphitheater edge. Night camping above 7,500 feet. This is a full cinematic episode.
shots: Horseshoe Bend: the classic wide-angle over the edge shot
Antelope Canyon: light beams through the slot (Upper Canyon in late morning)
Lake Powell/Glen Canyon Dam from the bridge
US-89 toward Kanab: Vermilion Cliffs glowing red
Bryce Canyon rim at golden hour — the hoodoos from above
Dispersed campsite with Milky Way over the canyon rim
facts: Horseshoe Bend is where the Colorado River makes a 270-degree incised meander around a fin of Navajo Sandstone — the rock you are standing on was laid down as seafloor 190 million years ago.
Antelope Canyon is the most photographed slot canyon in the world. The swirling walls were carved by flash floods over millions of years — a single flood can fill it 15 feet deep in minutes.

Bryce Canyon is not technically a canyon — it is a series of natural amphitheaters carved by erosion (frost-wedging and water), not cut by a river. The hoodoos are formed where soft and hard rock erode at different rates.

Lake Powell is the second-largest man-made reservoir in the United States. When Glen Canyon Dam was built in 1966, it flooded what many called the most beautiful stretch of the Colorado River — a debate about the dam rages among environmentalists to this day.

The Navajo Nation guided tour requirement for Antelope Canyon is not bureaucracy — the canyon is sacred Navajo land, and the tour system ensures that visitors understand what they are entering and who it belongs to.
days.culture: This day passes through three different Indigenous territories in a single riding day: Navajo Nation, ancestral Pueblo land, and Paiute territory. The landscape these cultures developed relationship with over thousands of years is the same one you ride through in hours. That contrast is worth sitting with and mentioning on camera.
talkingPoints: Antelope Canyon: how to actually get the light beam shots — tour timing matters
Glen Canyon: the most beautiful place in America that no longer exists — what the dam covered
Bryce Canyon hoodoos as alien architecture — and the science of how they form
Epic day realism: 500 km in desert heat with two major stops — hydration and pacing strategy
days.cta: Which sight would you prioritize on this day — Horseshoe Bend, Antelope Canyon, or Bryce? Make your case.

days.videoTitle: UT-12 to Moab — America's Most Scenic Highway to its Most Extreme Park | Day 14
days.hook: UT-12 is ranked the most scenic highway in America. I-70 through the desert is ranked one of the most desolate. Today, I ride both.
days.storyArc: UT-12 morning: slow curves through Capitol Reef, red rock domes, painted desert colors. Then I-70 east: one of the most remote Interstate stretches in the entire US system. Descend into Moab. Arches NP entrance — 2,000 natural arches, Delicate Arch, and one of the best adventure-riding destinations in the world.
shots: UT-12 hogsback section — two-lane road with 1,000-foot drops on both sides
Capitol Reef: white sandstone domes that inspired the name
I-70 remote stretch: no services for 100+ miles sign
Colorado River entry into Moab — wide canyon
Arches NP entrance: the gateway fins
Delicate Arch: hike or at least the viewpoint shot
facts: UT-12 has been called America's most scenic highway by National Geographic, Sunset Magazine, and Travel + Leisure simultaneously — it covers 124 miles through five different geological zones.
Arches National Park contains more than 2,000 natural sandstone arches — the highest known concentration on Earth. New ones form; old ones occasionally collapse (one fell in 1940, another in 2008).
Delicate Arch is 65 feet tall, stands on the rim of a bowl 500 feet above the Colorado River, and appears on the Utah state license plate — it is possibly the most recognizable natural arch on Earth.
The I-70 stretch between Green River and Salina, Utah (110 miles) has no services of any kind — it is one of the most desolate stretches of Interstate highway in the entire US system.
Canyonlands National Park near Moab was the filming location of the final scene of Thelma and Louise — the cliffs they drove over are directly identifiable.
days.culture: Moab has transformed from a uranium mining town to the mountain biking and off-road capital of America. It still has grit — you can feel the mining era in the bone structure of the town. The Jeep culture here is as strong as anywhere in the US. As a motorcycle rider, you belong to a tradition that goes back to the earliest wheeled explorers of slickrock country.
talkingPoints: UT-12 hogsback: the road with 1,000-foot drops on both sides — no barriers
Capitol Reef: the most undervisited 'big five' Utah park and why it's actually the best
Arches timed entry — reserve ahead on Recreation.gov or you wait outside
Dead Horse Point State Park as the premium viewpoint people miss
days.cta: What's the most beautiful road you've ever driven? Comment below — I'll feature the best answers.

days.videoTitle: Moab Zero Day — Arches, Dead Horse Point, and the Art of Slowing Down | Day 15
days.hook: I have been riding since Day 1. Today I don't ride. Today, I walk into the oldest stone on Earth and watch the sun rise through a hole in a cliff.
days.storyArc: The rare zero day. Arches National Park at dawn — Delicate Arch hike while the rock is orange and cool. Dead Horse Point SP mid-morning: 600 meters above the Colorado River, a horseshoe meander so perfect it looks fake. Moab town in the afternoon: the culture of an outdoor desert town, gear shops, resupply, local color. Evening: the reflection that only comes from stillness after weeks of motion.
shots: Pre-dawn departure from camp/host — the bike parked, a hiking bag on your back instead
Arches NP entrance sign at first light
Delicate Arch: the full hike — the surprise of walking over a slickrock ridge and suddenly seeing it
Framing yourself inside the arch — the classic composition
Colorado River from Dead Horse Point: the full horseshoe bend from 600 m above
Moab Main Street at midday: the outdoor-sports-town vibe (Gear shops, ice cream, other riders)
Evening: bike sitting in the garage / camp. Rider sitting still. No helmet. Just the desert air.
facts: Arches National Park contains more than 2,000 natural stone arches — the highest concentration of natural arches in the world. Delicate Arch, at 65 feet tall, is Utah's official state symbol and appears on the Utah license plate.

Delicate Arch was called 'Old Maid's Bloomers' by early settlers before being renamed. Edward Abbey, who worked as a park ranger at Arches in the 1950s, described it as 'a pair of … well, a large red sandstone arch' in his book Desert Solitaire — one of the most influential environmental books ever written.

Dead Horse Point gets its name from a legend that cowboys used the narrow neck of the mesa as a natural corral for wild mustangs — then allegedly abandoned a herd there without water. The mesa is only 30 meters wide at its narrowest neck.

The Colorado River visible from Dead Horse Point has carved through rock layers that are 300 million years old — the river itself is geologically young (5-6 million years). Water cutting through ancient stone over millions of years: this is what you are looking at.

Moab is the adventure sports capital of the American Southwest — it became famous for mountain biking in the late 1980s when the slickrock trail opened. The town has only ~5,000 residents but hosts hundreds of thousands of visitors annually. Film crews have used the surrounding landscape as a stand-in for Mars in multiple movies.
days.culture: The Moab area sits within the ancestral territory of the Ute people, whose descendants still live in Utah. The name 'Moab' may derive from the Biblical Moab (a land beyond the known world) or from a Paiute word. Edward Abbey's Desert Solitaire, written right here, created the philosophical underpinning of the American wilderness preservation movement. A zero day in this landscape — no deadlines, no km counter — is not laziness. It is the reason to take a long trip.
talkingPoints: Why zero days on a long trip are not wasted days — the physical and mental reset argument
Delicate Arch hike logistics: the 3.2-mile slickrock trail, why you go at dawn, what to carry
Dead Horse Point vs. Canyonlands: two world-class overlooks separated by a 30-minute drive
Moab's outdoor culture and why the DR650 fits perfectly in this town
Edward Abbey and Desert Solitaire: the book that made the desert political
days.cta: Have you ever taken a zero day mid-trip and felt guilty about it? Tell me how it turned out — in the comments below.

days.videoTitle: Zion at the Horizon — Riding the Desert South to St. George | Day 16
days.hook: I-15 south from Moab: the desert turns colors you don't have names for.
days.storyArc: Long desert run south and west. Zion approaches — the canyon wall visible from the highway. St. George as a desert outpost: Brigham Young's winter escape, now a booming retirement city. Find dispersed camp outside town as the sun drops behind the Virgin River Gorge.
shots: I-15 south: Virgin River Gorge slot — the highway cuts through vertical walls
Zion Canyon from US-9 or from the highway approach: the sandstone cliffs
St. George historic downtown: red brick, pioneer era
Brigham Young's winter home — preserved and open to visitors
Desert sunset over the BLM flats west of St. George
Red rock/sage brush color palette — wide establishing shot
facts: The Virgin River Gorge section of I-15 between Utah and Nevada is considered one of the most expensive sections of Interstate highway ever built per mile — the canyon walls required massive engineering.
Zion National Park's main tunnel (1.1 miles) was the longest highway tunnel in the United States when completed in 1930. Vehicles wider than 7'10'' require an escort — check before riding through.
St. George, Utah was established as a cotton-growing community in 1861 on orders from Brigham Young — it was known as 'Dixie' because of its warm climate and cotton ambitions.

The area around St. George has some of the highest density of dinosaur tracks in North America — the St. George Dinosaur Discovery Site contains more than 1,000 dinosaur tracks from the Early Jurassic period.
BLM (Bureau of Land Management) manages 245 million acres of American public land — more than any other federal agency — and much of it in southern Utah is open for dispersed camping.
days.culture: Southern Utah is deeply tied to LDS (Mormon) history and culture — St. George, Cedar City, and the surrounding 'Utah Dixie' were colonized by early LDS settlers on a mission from Brigham Young. Understanding this history gives context to the architecture, place names, and social landscape of the region. It's not intrusive — it's part of what makes this corner of America distinct.
talkingPoints: The Virgin River Gorge — a canyon you drive through at 70 mph that rivals any national park
Zion: the tunnel rule for wide vehicles and why it matters for loaded adventure bikes
Dispersed BLM camping near St. George — the gateway to free desert nights
Foreshadowing tomorrow: the Mojave Desert and California
days.cta: Tent camping or motel when you're solo-traveling? Tell me your preference and your reasoning.

days.videoTitle: Crossing the Mojave — The Desert That Tests Everything | Day 17
days.hook: The Mojave Desert in June. Pavement surface temperature: 65°C. Air temperature: 45°C. Engine temperature: climbing.
days.storyArc: Departure from St. George — the I-15 through Nevada's desert corridor. Valley of Fire: ancient red rock, the oldest in the region. Into California. Apple Valley arrival: suburban California adjacent to the Mojave. End the episode with a heat-management tutorial and the first whiff of Pacific air in the forecast.
shots: Valley of Fire NV entrance: blood-red Aztec sandstone
California state line: 'Welcome to California' sign — iconic
Temperature gauge or helmet thermometer reading
I-15 through the Mojave: heat shimmer on tarmac
Apple Valley: suburban streets, palm trees, mountains in background
Sunset over the Mojave: pink and purple gradients
facts: The Mojave Desert covers approximately 25,000 square miles across California, Nevada, Utah, and Arizona — it is the smallest and driest desert in North America.
Road surface temperature in the Mojave during June can exceed 65°C (150°F) — significantly hotter than air temperature. Tire pressure increases and tire compound softens in extreme heat.

Valley of Fire State Park in Nevada is the oldest state park in Nevada (established 1935) and contains rocks that are 150 million years old — some of the oldest exposed rock visible in the American Southwest.
Apple Valley, California was where Roy Rogers and Dale Evans lived for decades — their museum (now closed) was a major Western Americana destination.
The Joshua Tree is the signature plant of the Mojave Desert and can live for 500+ years — climate change is shifting its range northward and its long-term survival is a subject of active research.
days.culture: The Mojave is a cultural threshold. Drive east of it and you're in the interior West — rugged, self-reliant, conservative. Drive west and you're entering the California coastal culture — progressive, wealthy, globally connected. The desert is not just geological: it is the psychological and cultural buffer zone between two very different Americas.
talkingPoints: Heat management on a motorcycle: when to stop, how to hydrate, gear choices
Valley of Fire as the greatest state park nobody talks about
The two Californias: coastal vs. inland — entering from the desert, you see the real boundary
Tomorrow: Pacific Coast Highway. The most anticipated day of the trip.
days.cta: Have you ever ridden or driven in extreme heat? What's the highest temperature you've endured? Tell me below.

days.videoTitle: Pacific Ocean Arrived — PCH, Leo Carrillo, and Big Sur in One Epic Day | Day 18
days.hook: I have been riding toward the Pacific for 17 days. Today I smell salt water. Then I keep riding — along the most famous coastline in America — until I camp above the ocean on a cliff with no guardrails and no cell service.
days.storyArc: The day the continent opens up. Barstow to LA: navigate the California freeway system (a culture note for Canadian/international viewers on lane filtering). Then the emotional rupture: the Pacific Ocean in the windshield. Leo Carrillo State Beach (Malibu): park the bike, walk into the water, close the circle from Day 9's Route 66 midpoint. Then north on PCH: Santa Barbara, Morro Bay, the Big Sur cliffs, Bixby Creek Bridge. Prewitt Ridge at dusk: a rough dirt road climbs 1,000 feet above Hwy 1 to a dispersed camp on a cliff above the ocean. Stars and wave-sounds all night.
shots: Barstow departure — 6:30 am, pre-dawn desert light
CA-14 into the LA basin: the Valley approach, the smog horizon
First Pacific Ocean sight from PCH — pull over and react on camera (this is the clip)
Santa Monica Pier — the 'End of the Trail' Route 66 sign. This closes the Day 9 arc
Leo Carrillo day stop: boots off, standing in the Pacific, GoPro rolling
PCH riding footage: cliff one side, open ocean the other — this is why people ride motorcycles
Bixby Creek Bridge from the cliff above — the signature Big Sur arch-over-canyon shot
McWay Falls: waterfall directly onto the beach (Julia Pfeiffer Burns SP)
Prewitt Ridge dirt road climb — the turn-off and the rough ascent
Camp: edge of the cliff, ocean 1,000 feet below, sunset, tent
facts: Pacific Coast Highway (Highway 1) was built partially using prison labor in the 1930s — considered one of the most dangerous construction projects in California history. Multiple sections have since slid into the ocean and been rebuilt.
Santa Monica Pier marks the western terminus of Route 66 — the 'End of the Trail' sign is at the end of the pier. If you rode any part of Route 66 on Day 9, standing here closes a 2,400-mile arc.

Leo Carrillo State Beach is named after actor and conservationist Leo Carrillo, who spent 18 years lobbying to keep California's beaches publicly accessible. His efforts directly shaped California beach law — most beaches in the state must remain publicly accessible because of the California Coastal Act he influenced.

Big Sur is approximately 90 miles long with no town, no chain restaurants, and no reliable cell service for most of its length. This is deliberate: residents have repeatedly defeated development proposals since the 1960s.

Bixby Creek Bridge is 714 feet long and 260 feet above the canyon — when completed in 1932 it was one of the longest single-span concrete bridges in the world. It has appeared in more car advertisements and movies than almost any other bridge in the US.

Prewitt Ridge Road is a BLM access road that climbs 1,000+ feet above Highway 1 to dispersed camping sites — known primarily to locals and overlanders, with arguably the most dramatic camping view in California.

California is the only US state with explicitly legal lane filtering (splitting) — California DMV/CHP guidelines define the speed differential and positioning rules. Check CHP's current guidelines before filtering.
days.culture: Arriving at the Pacific after crossing the continent carries a weight that can't be manufactured. The continent is behind you. You are as far west as the road goes. Leo Carrillo is your Pacific arrival moment — budget time here. Big Sur, meanwhile, has attracted artists, writers, and spiritual seekers since the 1940s. The Esalen Institute (founded 1962) is literally built into these cliffs and invented the human potential movement. Henry Miller lived here from 1944–1962. Riding through it on a loaded bike, without a deadline, is exactly the spirit it was built to reward.
talkingPoints: The Route 66 arc closes: midpoint sign in Adrian, Texas on Day 9 — the end sign at Santa Monica today
California lane filtering: what it actually is, what CHP says, how to do it safely on a loaded bike
Leo Carrillo as a day stop vs. overnight: why the extra 300 km to Big Sur is worth it
The psychological reset of ocean after 17 days of desert and mountains
Prewitt Ridge logistics: the turn-off, the road condition, what to carry, arrive before dark
Big Sur's deliberate rejection of commercialization — why it matters and who fights for it
days.cta: What was the moment in your life where you finally made it somewhere you'd been riding toward? Tell me the story below.

days.videoTitle: The Lost Coast — Riding California's Wild North | Day 19
days.hook: Point Arena Lighthouse is the nearest point in the continental United States to Hawaii. It is also where the San Andreas Fault comes ashore.
days.storyArc: Continue north on California's coast — past Monterey, past the vineyards, into the Mendocino coast's wild weather. The 'Lost Coast': so rugged that the highway engineers gave up and left it wild. Point Arena Lighthouse as the episode's anchor: two world-record facts in one structure. Fog, cold marine air, the GPS pin at the edge of the continent.
shots: Monterey Bay from the highway — sea otters in the kelp if you're lucky
Mendocino village: Victorian buildings on a headland above the Pacific
Sonoma Coast: cliffs and sea stacks at sunset
Point Arena Lighthouse: 115 feet tall, white tower on a headland
Looking out from Point Arena toward Hawaii (hint at distance on camera)
GPS coordinates on phone: 38.9551° N, 123.7399° W
facts: Point Arena Lighthouse stands at the point of the California coast nearest to the Hawaiian Islands — Hawaii is approximately 2,070 miles due west.

The original Point Arena Lighthouse was destroyed in the 1906 San Francisco Earthquake — Point Arena was one of the most severely affected regions of the 1906 quake. The current tower was rebuilt in 1908.
The San Andreas Fault comes ashore at Point Arena — the Pacific Plate and North American Plate meet here in a visible geological expression. You can walk along the fault line.
Mendocino was used as the filming location for the entire run of Murder She Wrote (1984–1996) because it photographed convincingly as a Maine fishing village — residents found this endlessly amusing.

The 'Lost Coast' of northern California was so geologically unstable and expensive to build roads through that highway engineers simply routed Highway 1 inland — it remains one of the most remote stretches of California coastline, accessible only by rugged mountain roads.
days.culture: The Mendocino coast has a specific counter-culture history — it became a haven for 1960s back-to-the-land homesteaders and artists who found Big Sur too crowded. Many of those communities are still there, three generations in. The culture is organic, DIY, and deeply attached to the land and sea.
talkingPoints: The San Andreas Fault: walking the most dangerous geological feature in North America
The 1906 earthquake's true geographic reach — not just San Francisco
Murder She Wrote's fake Maine: how TV shapes our perception of places we've never been
Riding the Lost Coast: why the highway bypassed it and what that preserved
days.cta: What earthquake zone or natural hazard site have you visited? Tell me how it made you feel.

days.videoTitle: Into the Redwoods — The Tallest Living Things on Earth | Day 20
days.hook: These trees were alive when the Roman Empire fell. And they are still growing.
days.storyArc: US-101 north into Humboldt County. The trees beginning to appear: first tall, then enormous, then incomprehensible in scale. Avenue of the Giants: ride it slowly, with the camera pointed up. Arrive at the Redwood National and State Parks area. The elk at dusk on the roadway. The forest absorbs sound — true silence.
shots: Avenue of the Giants approach: cathedral canopy forming above the road
Stop and look up: 380-foot redwood from below
Bike parked beside a coast redwood: scale comparison
Hand touching bark: 12-inch thick, deeply furrowed
Coastal elk herd on US-101 at dusk — pull over
The light in the old-growth forest: green, filtered, ancient
facts: Coast redwoods are the tallest living things on Earth — the tallest known individual (Hyperion, in Redwood National Park) stands 380.3 feet (115.92 meters). Its exact location is kept secret to protect it.
The oldest living coast redwoods are approximately 2,200 years old — the same trees existed when Julius Caesar was born.
Redwood bark can be 12 inches thick — it contains tannins that make it naturally fire-resistant and largely immune to insects. This is why so many have survived millennia.
Redwood trees create their own micro-weather. In the summer, when no rain falls, they absorb fog through their needles — a single large tree can extract 40 gallons of water from fog per day.
Avenue of the Giants is a 31-mile stretch of the original US-101 highway running through Humboldt Redwoods State Park — it contains the largest remaining old-growth coast redwood forest in the world.
days.culture: The redwood forests carry a specific spiritual weight for many people who visit — the scale of the trees is literally incomprehensible to human brains calibrated for human-scale objects. Many visitors become quiet and still in old-growth redwood groves, as if in a cathedral. This is worth acknowledging on camera: let your audience feel that moment, not just hear about it.
talkingPoints: The Hyperion tree: the tallest living thing and why its location is secret
How redwoods make their own rain — the fog-drip system
The elk on US-101: a reminder that this ecosystem is still functioning at scale
What it feels like to stand next to a living 2,000-year-old organism
days.cta: What's the oldest or largest natural thing you've stood next to? Tell me what it felt like.

days.videoTitle: California to Oregon — Riding Toward Crater Lake | Day 21
days.hook: Oregon has more ghost towns than any other state. It also has the clearest lake in North America. Today, I try to reach both.
days.storyArc: Cross the California-Oregon border — the landscape shifts: Douglas fir, wetter, greener, more rugged. US-199 through the Siskiyou Mountains. Oregon Caves if time allows. The Crater Lake detour question: it's worth it, but it adds significant mileage. Arrive at Westfir — a small timber town in the Cascades.
shots: 'Welcome to Oregon' sign — clean, green, forest behind it
US-199 Siskiyou mountain curves: tight, wooded, dramatic
Oregon ghost town if you find one roadside
Crater Lake if you detour: the blue — shocking, unreal blue
Old-growth Douglas fir corridor approaching Westfir
Westfir: timber town quiet, single main road, classic small-town Oregon
facts: Crater Lake is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,943 feet (592 meters) — it is so deep that the bottom has never been touched by sunlight.

Crater Lake was formed approximately 7,700 years ago when Mount Mazama, a 12,000-foot stratovolcano, erupted and collapsed into its own magma chamber — the eruption was 42 times more powerful than the 1980 Mount St. Helens eruption.
The water in Crater Lake is so clear and pure (it receives no inflowing rivers, only rain and snowmelt) that you can see 143 feet deep — it is the clearest lake in North America.

The Klamath people knew of Crater Lake for at least 10,000 years before Euro-American settlers. They called it 'Giiwas' and considered it a sacred site — their oral traditions describe the eruption that created it.
Oregon has more than 400 recognized ghost towns — the highest count of any US state — a legacy of boom-bust cycles in gold, silver, timber, and fishing.
days.culture: Oregon is a study in contrasts: the most progressive coastal cities (Portland, Eugene, Corvallis) and deeply conservative rural areas in the east and south, all connected by spectacular landscape. The timber culture in places like Westfir is authentic and fading — the mills that built these towns have closed and the communities are reinventing themselves slowly.
talkingPoints: Crater Lake blue: the science of why water that deep and clear looks that color
The 7,700-year-old eruption: how oral traditions of the Klamath people describe it
Oregon ghost towns: what they tell us about resource extraction economics
Timber culture: the identity of a region whose primary industry has disappeared
days.cta: Crater Lake: worth the detour? Have you been? Did the color actually look that blue in person?

days.videoTitle: Columbia River Gorge — Wind, Water, and the World's Biggest Floods | Day 22
days.hook: The Columbia River Gorge was carved by the most catastrophic floods in Earth's recorded geological history. And today I ride through it.
days.storyArc: Westfir north to the Columbia River. The landscape opening up: the Cascades giving way to the Gorge. Multnomah Falls if time allows. The wind: Hood River is a world windsurfing capital because the Gorge acts as a natural wind tunnel. Goldendale, WA: arriving at the state line, the last rays of sun over the plateau.
shots: Columbia River first sight: wide, silver, industrial-scale
Historic Columbia River Highway cornices and cliff-edge road
Multnomah Falls: 620 feet, two-tiered — Oregon's most-visited natural attraction
Hood River: windsurfers and kitesurfers on the Columbia
Bridge of the Gods at Cascade Locks — stepping stone to Washington
Goldendale, WA: high plateau, wide sky, Cascades behind
facts: The Columbia River Gorge was carved primarily during the Missoula Floods — a series of approximately 40 catastrophic megafloods between 15,000 and 13,000 years ago. A single flood released more water than all the world's rivers combined.
Multnomah Falls drops 620 feet in two tiers and is the second-tallest year-round waterfall in the United States — it receives about 2 million visitors per year.

The Historic Columbia River Highway (completed 1916) was the first planned scenic highway in the United States — it was specifically designed to showcase the Gorge's beauty, with engineered viewpoints and graceful curves.
Hood River, Oregon is one of the top three windsurfing destinations in the world. The Gorge funnels wind at consistent 15-35 mph speeds through a corridor that aligns perfectly for the sport.

The Bridge of the Gods at Cascade Locks is named after a Native American legend describing a natural land bridge across the Columbia that collapsed — geologists believe a large landslide may have actually temporarily dammed the river here around 1450 CE.
days.culture: The Gorge is a place of deep Native American history — the Celilo Falls on the Columbia River (now submerged under The Dalles Dam) was one of the most important fishing sites in all of North America for 15,000 years, used by tribes from across the continent. When the dam flooded it in 1957, the event was mourned as a cultural catastrophe by Indigenous people throughout the Pacific Northwest.
talkingPoints: The Missoula Floods: the most extreme weather event in North American human history
Celilo Falls: the irreplaceable cultural site that was deliberately drowned in 1957
Wind in the Gorge: why Hood River is a world windsurfing capital
The Bridge of the Gods legend and the geology behind it
days.cta: What's the most geologically dramatic place you've ever been? The thing that made you realize how temporary we are?

days.videoTitle: Portland to Seattle — Riding Through the Birthplace of American Counterculture | Day 23
days.hook: Portland has more independent bookstores per capita than any other US city. Seattle gave the world Starbucks, Nirvana, and Amazon. I'm riding between them.
days.storyArc: Mosier to Portland on I-84. Portland: the 'Keep Portland Weird' culture, Powell's Books, Mount Hood in the distance. I-5 north through the heart of Cascadia. Mount St. Helens visible from the highway. Seattle arrival: the mountain views, the water, the density of history in this young city.
shots: Portland: Powell's Books exterior — 'City of Books'
Mount Hood from the Portland skyline corridor
I-5 north: Mount St. Helens' truncated summit visible
Vancouver WA / Oregon border: Columbia River bridge
Seattle arrival: Space Needle, downtown, Elliott Bay
Pike Place Market: the fish throw — classic Seattle content
facts: Mount St. Helens erupted on May 18, 1980, killing 57 people and reducing the summit by 1,313 feet in a single morning. The eruption ejected 1 cubic mile of material and was heard in 11 states.
Pike Place Market in Seattle has operated continuously since August 17, 1907, making it the oldest continuously operating public farmers market in the United States.

Seattle has been the birthplace of Boeing (1916), REI (1938), Starbucks (1971), Microsoft (1975), Amazon (1994), and was the epicenter of grunge music (1990–1994) — an extraordinary concentration of cultural and economic innovation for a city its size.
Portland, Oregon has more breweries per capita than any other city in the United States and arguably the world — over 70 within city limits.
The Cascades form a literal rain shadow: the west side of the mountains receives over 80 inches of rain per year; the east side (where you came from) receives under 12 inches.
days.culture: The Pacific Northwest has a very specific identity: environmentally conscious, technically innovative, politically progressive, and deeply attached to outdoor culture. The craft beer, independent coffee, outdoor gear, and tech startup cultures that now exist globally were largely invented or refined in the Portland-Seattle corridor. Arriving on a motorcycle from the interior West, you can feel the cultural shift.
talkingPoints: Why Seattle produced so many iconic companies and cultural movements in such a short time
Grunge: what it actually was, where it came from, and why it matters as cultural history
Mount St. Helens: riding past a mountain that was 1,300 feet taller 45 years ago
Pike Place Market fish throw: why it's worth stopping despite being a tourist cliché
days.cta: Seattle, Portland, or Vancouver — best Pacific Northwest city? Fight it out in the comments.

days.videoTitle: North Cascades — America's Most Remote Highway | Day 24
days.hook: State Route 20 through the North Cascades is only open about half the year. Today, it's open. And I'm on it.
days.storyArc: Seattle departure. The anticipation of the North Cascades Highway — check it's open (it was). The transformation as you climb: the city behind, the peaks ahead. Ross Lake. Washington Pass. The descent into Winthrop: the deliberate Old West town. The Cascades are America's Alps — say it on camera.
shots: SR-20 east of Marblemount: the road disappearing into mountains
Gorge Lake from the dam: deep glacier-blue water
Washington Pass Overlook: Liberty Bell Mountain spire
The descent toward Winthrop: valley opening, brown hills, Old West
Winthrop main street: deliberately preserved Western frontier architecture
Campsite near Twisp or Winthrop: Cascades in every direction
facts: The North Cascades Highway (SR-20) typically closes from November to mid-April due to avalanche risk and snow accumulation — it is one of the last highways in Washington to open each spring.
The North Cascades contain more glaciers than any other mountain range in the contiguous United States — over 300 active glaciers, though they are retreating rapidly.
Washington Pass sits at 5,477 feet and offers views of Liberty Bell Mountain, a granitic spire that has been climbed by some of the most accomplished alpine climbers in America.

Winthrop, Washington deliberately preserved its Western frontier architectural aesthetic in 1972 after the North Cascades Highway opened — the entire downtown was redesigned as an Old West theme to attract tourism. It worked.
The North Cascades have been called 'the American Alps' by Ansel Adams and others — the combination of granite peaks, permanent snowfields, and deep valleys is genuinely alpine in character.
days.culture: Eastern Washington is a completely different culture from the Seattle side of the mountains — agriculturally dominated, politically conservative, and with a proud independence from 'west-siders.' Winthrop's Old West identity is a deliberate invention that succeeded — but the ranching and orchard culture of the Methow Valley around it is entirely real.
talkingPoints: The seasonal highway: how to check opening dates and why it still closes in 2026
North Cascades glaciers: more than the Alps and disappearing faster
Winthrop's invented Old West: why manufactured authenticity sometimes works
The rain shadow transition: crossing the Cascades is like entering a different country
days.cta: What's the most spectacular mountain pass you've ever crossed? I want to see photos and stories.

days.videoTitle: Eastern Washington to Spokane — Where the West Becomes the Plateau | Day 25
days.hook: Nobody talks about eastern Washington. That's exactly why it's worth talking about.
days.storyArc: Winthrop east through the Okanagan valley into the Columbia Plateau. The landscape: sage, wheat, basalt, sky. Grand Coulee Dam if the detour works. Spokane arrival — a city with more character than its reputation suggests. The city literally built around its waterfalls.
shots: Eastern WA wheat fields: golden, rolling, quiet
Columbia River Basin: dramatic basalt coulees
Grand Coulee Dam if detour: scale of it — the largest hydroelectric power plant in the US
Spokane downtown: the falls visible from the city center, bridges above
Riverfront Park: 1974 World's Fair site, still active
Spokane Valley suburbs: the other side of the Pacific Northwest
facts: Grand Coulee Dam, completed in 1942, was the largest concrete structure ever built at that time — it is still the largest hydroelectric power plant in the United States and the fifth-largest in the world.
Spokane hosted the 1974 World's Fair — Expo '74 — which was the first World's Fair focused entirely on environmental themes. It transformed the city's downtown and created Riverfront Park.
Spokane was the first city west of the Mississippi River to have an electric street railway system (1889) — partly because the Spokane River provided cheap hydroelectric power from day one.
Eastern Washington grows approximately 20% of the total US apple crop — the Wenatchee and Chelan valleys are among the most productive apple-growing regions on Earth.

The Channeled Scablands of eastern Washington — the basalt plateau cut into coulees and cliffs — were created by the same Missoula Floods that carved the Columbia River Gorge. The entire region was shaped by catastrophic Ice Age floods.
days.culture: Spokane has a working-class confidence that bigger cities sometimes lack. It has produced remarkable people (Bing Crosby was from here), hosted world events, and sits in a landscape of genuinely dramatic geology that most Americans have never seen or thought about. Arrive without assumptions.
talkingPoints: The Channeled Scablands: the ground-level evidence of the Ice Age megafloods
Grand Coulee Dam: what the largest structure in the Western Hemisphere actually looks like up close
Spokane's identity: a city that had to reinvent itself after the timber and mining booms ended
Bing Crosby: yes, that Bing Crosby — from here
days.cta: What city surprised you the most — the one you expected nothing from and got everything? Tell me.

days.videoTitle: Lolo Pass — Riding the Road Lewis & Clark Walked | Day 26
days.hook: In 1805, Lewis & Clark crossed these mountains on foot. It almost killed them. Today, I do it in a day on a motorcycle.
days.storyArc: Spokane to the Clearwater corridor on US-12. The Nez Percé homeland. Lochsa River: one of the best whitewater rivers in America. Lolo Pass. The last road in the lower 48 to be paved (1962). Arrival at Missoula/Frenchtown: the Bitterroot Valley beginning.
shots: US-12 entering Idaho: Clearwater River gorge begins
Lochsa River white water alongside the road
Lolo Pass summit: Continental Divide sign
Lewis & Clark historic markers along the route
Montana state line: 'Welcome to Montana — Big Sky Country'
Frenchtown or Missoula evening: broad valley, Clark Fork River
facts: US-12 through the Lochsa River canyon was the last major road in the contiguous United States to be paved — construction was completed in 1962. Before that, this was essentially wilderness.
Lolo Pass (5,233 feet) is on the Continental Divide — water falling on the east side eventually reaches the Gulf of Mexico; water on the west side reaches the Pacific Ocean.

The Nez Percé people were exceptional horsemen who developed the Appaloosa breed — their horses were so superior that early explorers credited the Nez Percé with more sophisticated horse culture than any other Plains tribe.

Lewis & Clark crossed Lolo Pass in September 1805 — they were so weakened by starvation in the mountains that Clark wrote in his journal they were 'compelled to kill a colt for our supper.' They nearly didn't make it.

The Lochsa River is one of the premier whitewater rivers in the United States — its Selway-Bitterroot Wilderness section is accessible only on foot or by raft and is one of the most pristine river systems in North America.
days.culture: The Clearwater and Lochsa corridor is in the heart of Nez Percé territory — the tribe fought a remarkable 1,170-mile fighting retreat in 1877, outwitting US Army forces for months before being caught 40 miles from the Canadian border. Their story is one of the most extraordinary chapters of American history, largely untaught. Riding through their homeland knowing this changes how it feels.
talkingPoints: Lewis & Clark's near-death crossing: what the journals actually say about this passage
The Nez Percé 1877 retreat: 800 people, 2,000 horses, 40 miles from freedom
The Appaloosa breed: one of the most significant contributions to American horsemanship
The last road to be paved in America — what it means that this was still wilderness in 1962
days.cta: What's the most historically significant road or trail you've traveled? Tell me the history you learned.

days.videoTitle: Into Yellowstone Country — The Volcano Beneath Montana | Day 27
days.hook: Under your wheels for the next two days: the world's most active supervolcano. It last erupted 640,000 years ago. It will erupt again.
days.storyArc: Missoula/Frenchtown south through the Bitterroot Valley. Big Sky country: the sky getting taller, the land emptying out. Gallatin Gateway: the door to Yellowstone. The bison on the roadway before you even enter the park. End the episode in anticipation — tomorrow is Yellowstone.
shots: Bitterroot Valley south: mountains flanking a broad ranching valley
Montana ranch gates and property signs: Big Sky identity
Bison on the roadway — first sighting, stop the bike
Gallatin River corridor: fly-fishing, drift boats, 'A River Runs Through It' country
Gallatin Gateway: the old railroad hotel, the mountains ahead
First Yellowstone NP entrance sign — wide, anticipatory
facts: Montana has the fourth lowest population density in the United States — approximately 7 people per square mile — and is the fourth largest state by area.

The Yellowstone Supervolcano's magma chamber is approximately 37 miles by 18 miles in surface area. Its last three major eruptions were 2.1 million, 1.3 million, and 640,000 years ago. The cycle suggests another major eruption is 'overdue.'

Bison were reduced from an estimated 60 million animals in 1800 to approximately 300 by 1889 — systematic hunting was used deliberately as a strategy to undermine the Plains Indian cultures dependent on them. Yellowstone is where the only continuously wild American bison herd survived.

The Gallatin River, which parallels the road from Bozeman south, was the setting for Norman Maclean's 1976 novella 'A River Runs Through It' — the 1992 film was shot here and established the fly-fishing cultural identity of Montana globally.

Montana had no daytime speed limit from 1995 to 1999 — during that period the law required only 'reasonable and prudent' speed. It was the only state in the modern era to have no numerical speed limit.
days.culture: Montana's identity is built on specific myths: ranching, fly-fishing, wilderness, and a rugged independence that sometimes tips into libertarianism. The reality is that Montana is also a deeply Indigenous land (the Crow, Blackfeet, Flathead, and Cheyenne nations all have major reservations here) and is experiencing profound economic change as tech workers and retirees move in, pricing out generational families.
talkingPoints: The supervolcano context: why Yellowstone is geologically unlike any other park in the world
Bison near-extinction and recovery: the most successful wildlife conservation story in American history
'A River Runs Through It' and the cultural construction of Montana's fly-fishing identity
The changing Montana: who lives here now and what it's doing to the culture
days.cta: If you could spend one week anywhere in Montana, where would it be? I'm taking suggestions for future trips.

days.videoTitle: Inside Yellowstone — Old Faithful, Grand Prismatic, and Why This Park Is Unlike Any Other | Day 28
days.hook: Half of all the geothermal features on Earth are in this one park. Yellowstone is not just a park. It is a geological argument about the nature of the planet.
days.storyArc: Full day inside Yellowstone. Structure the episode around the three acts: geothermal wonders (Old Faithful, Grand Prismatic), wildlife (Hayden Valley, Lamar Valley), and geological overview (what the supervolcano means). The canyon. The quieter moments. End with the visceral feeling of camping or staying near a place this raw.
shots: Old Faithful eruption: the full arc from build-up to peak
Grand Prismatic Spring from the overlook: color bands — blue, green, yellow, orange, red
Hayden Valley: bison herd crossing, the sheer number of animals
Yellowstone Grand Canyon: the Lower Falls, yellow/orange canyon walls
Lamar Valley at dawn: the most wildlife-dense temperate valley in North America
Geothermal steam vents at road level: the scale of the heat below
facts: Old Faithful erupts approximately every 44 to 125 minutes (it varies with earthquake activity) and has been erupting continuously for at least 30,000 years.

Yellowstone contains approximately 500 geysers — more than half of all the geysers on Earth. The next three largest concentrations are in Chile, Iceland, and New Zealand — combined they equal Yellowstone's count.

The colors of Grand Prismatic Spring come from species of heat-adapted bacteria and archaea living in specific temperature zones. The spring is 370 feet across and the third-largest hot spring in the world.

The Yellowstone caldera is 30 miles by 45 miles — the ground above it bulges and sinks measurably every year. The park sits 2,000 feet above the surrounding plateau because of the upward pressure of the magma below.

Yellowstone was established in 1872 as the world's first national park — the concept of preserving wilderness for public enjoyment rather than private exploitation was genuinely novel and was watched with skepticism globally.
days.culture: Yellowstone is where the American national park idea was born and where it is most tested. The park has one of the highest densities of wildlife-related visitor injuries in the US system — people consistently underestimate the wildness of the animals because they're accustomed to zoo-scale distance. The bison will stop your motorcycle. The bears do not care about your presence. The geysers erupt whether or not you are in the way.
talkingPoints: Old Faithful timing: how to arrive for the best chance of a short wait for the next eruption
Grand Prismatic from the overlook vs. from the boardwalk: why altitude matters for that photo
Bison etiquette on motorcycle: what to actually do when one blocks the road
The world's first national park: what that concept meant in 1872 and what it still means
days.cta: What would you most want to see in Yellowstone — geysers, wildlife, or the canyon? Vote below.

days.videoTitle: Beartooth Highway — Charles Kuralt Called It the Most Beautiful Drive in America | Day 29
days.hook: This road hits 10,947 feet. Snow in June. Hairpin switchbacks. No barriers. Charles Kuralt was right.
days.storyArc: Exit Yellowstone via Northeast Entrance → Cooke City on US-212 — not the WY-310 / Chief Joseph shortcut toward Cody if your goal is the Beartooth. The climb: switchbacks, the horizon widening with every turn. The plateau at nearly 11,000 feet: a moonscape of rock, snow, and sky. The descent toward Red Lodge. The emotional release of having crossed the highest point of the entire trip — then roll on into Wyoming toward Cowley for the night.
shots: The Beartooth approach: gradual climb, then sudden exposure
Top-of-Beartooth plateau: snowfields in June, tundra, no trees
Switchbacks from above: the road zigzagging down the cliff face
Temperature gauge at the top: likely 5–10°C even in June
Red Lodge Montana at the base: Victorian downtown, relief of lower altitude
Looking back up at the Beartooth from Red Lodge: the scale of the climb
facts: The Beartooth Highway (US-212) reaches 10,947 feet (3,337 meters) at its highest point — it is the highest paved road you can reach in a standard vehicle in the entire United States.

Charles Kuralt, the CBS News journalist famous for his 'On the Road' segments, called the Beartooth Highway 'the most beautiful drive in America' — this designation has become the road's permanent marketing identity.
The Beartooth is typically open from late May to mid-October. During winter, it is buried under up to 25 feet of snow.
The plateau at the top of the Beartooth is high alpine tundra — the same ecosystem found in northern Canada and Alaska. Plants here grow a few millimeters per year.

Red Lodge, Montana was a coal mining town with a significant immigrant population — its Heritage Festival celebrates Scandinavian, Finnish, German, Italian, Scottish, and other traditions from its mining era.
days.culture: The Beartooth has a motorcycle culture unlike almost anywhere else — it's considered a bucket-list ride in the American motorcycle touring world, and you will share it with groups from across the country who have ridden hundreds of miles specifically to experience it. The camaraderie at the top is spontaneous and real.
talkingPoints: Navigation reality: why Google often pushes WY-310 — and why you force US-212 through Cooke City and Red Lodge for the real Beartooth
Cold management at altitude: gear layers, body temperature, fuel-range changes at elevation
The visual scale of the Beartooth plateau: why it looks like a different planet
Red Lodge's immigrant heritage: the diversity that mining brought to the American frontier
The Beartooth in June: snow possible, ice possible — check WSDOT/Montana DOT before going
days.cta: What's the highest elevation you've ever been on a vehicle — car, motorcycle, anything? Tell me the number.

days.videoTitle: Cowley to Gillette — Crossing Wyoming’s Wind and Coal Country | Day 30
days.hook: Wyoming has 580,000 people. It is the tenth largest state in America. That's about 5.8 people per square mile. You will feel every one of those empty miles.
days.storyArc: Morning in Cowley after the Beartooth push — you’re already in the Bighorn Basin. Roll out toward the Powder River: long slabs, crosswinds, and the scale of empty Wyoming. Gillette arrival: the energy capital of America, not the Wyoming anyone imagines.
shots: Wyoming plains from the highway: horizon in every direction, no hills
Wind farm: hundreds of turbines — Wyoming has the greatest wind energy potential in the US
Coal mine / strip mine if visible roadside near Gillette
Gillette downtown: working-class energy town, not tourist country
Wind data: show what 40 mph crosswind looks like physically on the bike
facts: Wyoming has the fewest residents of any US state — approximately 580,000 people, less than the city of Louisville, Kentucky.
Wyoming was the first government in the world to grant women the right to vote — the Wyoming Territory did so in 1869, 51 years before the 19th Amendment to the US Constitution.
The Powder River Basin near Gillette, Wyoming produces more coal than any other region in the United States — it accounts for approximately 40% of total US coal production.
