Wyoming has the greatest wind energy potential of any state in the US and is already a major wind power producer — the same plains that give coal also give wind.
The first all-female jury in US history sat in Laramie, Wyoming in 1870 — one year after women were given the vote in the territory.
days.culture: Wyoming's identity is paradoxical: deeply conservative culturally, yet historically the most progressive in terms of women's rights. Deeply tied to fossil fuels, yet sitting on arguably the country's greatest renewable energy resources. Gillette is a working energy town — it is not a tourist destination, and it doesn't pretend to be. That honesty is worth respecting.
talkingPoints: The wind as a physical riding experience: technique, fatigue, and respect for crosswinds
Wyoming's women's suffrage history: why it happened first here, of all places
The Powder River Coal Basin: the place that fuels American electricity and what comes next
Wyoming's paradox: fossil fuel culture and renewable energy potential in the same landscape
days.cta: What's the windiest riding condition you've experienced? How did you handle it? Share your technique.

days.videoTitle: Needles Highway — The Most Dramatic 14 Miles in the Black Hills | Day 31
days.hook: The Needles Highway has tunnels so narrow you fold your mirrors and hold your breath. It was built in 1922 by a governor who hated the idea of Mount Rushmore.
days.storyArc: Gillette west to the Black Hills. The geology shift: suddenly, granite spires erupting from the plains. Needles Highway: the tunnels, the tight turns, the 'Eye of the Needle' rock formation. Custer State Park bison. The Black Hills context: sacred Lakota land, still disputed, still powerful. End at a dispersed camp under ponderosa pines.
shots: Gillette exit: last view of the WY plains before the Black Hills
Black Hills granite spires beginning to appear
Needles Highway tunnel: the bike barely fits — camera inside the tunnel
'Eye of the Needle' spire against the sky
Custer State Park bison herd: 1,400 animals loose across the park
Iron Mountain Road if time: another signature Black Hills motorcycle road
facts: The Needles Highway covers 14 miles through narrow granite spires in Custer State Park — it includes tunnels barely wide enough for a single vehicle, with the narrowest being 8 feet 4 inches wide and 12 feet 1 inch tall.
South Dakota Governor Peter Norbeck, who had the Needles Highway built in 1922, actively opposed the carving of Mount Rushmore — he preferred to keep the Hills wild.

Mount Rushmore took 14 years to build (1927–1941), cost approximately $1 million, and is not finished — sculptor Gutzon Borglum had planned a Hall of Records behind Lincoln's head that was never completed. Lincoln's outstretched hand was also never finished.

In 1980, the US Supreme Court ruled that the Black Hills were taken illegally from the Lakota Sioux in violation of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty. The court awarded $106 million in compensation. The Sioux refused to accept the money — they want the land. The award, with interest, is now worth over $1.5 billion, sitting in a federal account unclaimed.
Custer State Park maintains one of the largest publicly owned bison herds in the world — approximately 1,400 animals roam freely across the park's 71,000 acres.
days.culture: The Black Hills are Pahá Sápa to the Lakota — 'the heart of everything that is.' The carving of Mt. Rushmore onto the most sacred mountains in Lakota cosmology was deliberate. The unclaimed $1.5 billion settlement is the most powerful statement the Sioux Nation could make. For a YouTube audience, this is not a political controversy — it is a story about what nations choose to hold onto and why.
talkingPoints: The Needles Highway tunnels: the physical sensation of threading a motorcycle through 8-foot granite
The $1.5 billion unclaimed: what the Lakota refusal to take money means and why it should be famous
Mount Rushmore: what Gutzon Borglum planned and never built
Custer State Park bison: 1,400 animals, no fence — what to do when they're on the road
days.cta: What do you think the right outcome for the Black Hills dispute is? Tell me honestly — I'll engage with every reply.

days.videoTitle: 600 km Across the Great Plains — Oacoma to Rochester | Day 32
days.hook: Day 32. 600 kilometers. Straight roads. You will discover things about yourself on a day like this.
days.storyArc: This is the introspective episode. The long day, the repetitive landscape, the mental game of big-mileage riding. Oacoma and the Missouri River crossing — the last major hydrological boundary. Minnesota: suddenly greener, the sky full of lakes. Rochester as the destination: a city that exists entirely because of one hospital.
shots: Missouri River crossing near Oacoma: massive, wide, brown water
'Welcome to Minnesota — Land of 10,000 Lakes' sign
Great Plains: the single most honest frame of the journey — straight road, sky
Minnesota lakes visible from the road: reflective, quiet
Rochester, MN: Mayo Clinic campus — the world's most famous hospital
Odometer shot: 32 days of riding logged
facts: The Missouri River is the longest river in the United States at 2,341 miles — longer than the Mississippi, though it empties into the Mississippi near St. Louis. Lewis & Clark followed it for most of their westward journey.

The Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota is the world's first and largest integrated nonprofit medical group practice — it was founded in 1863 following a tornado that killed 37 people. The Mayo brothers and the Sisters of Saint Francis built it from scratch.

Rochester, Minnesota exists almost entirely because of the Mayo Clinic — the clinic employs approximately 40,000 people in a city of about 125,000. It is one of the most monoculturally dominated cities in America.
Minnesota has more shoreline than California, Florida, and Hawaii combined — the state contains approximately 11,842 lakes of 10 acres or larger.

The Oacoma/Chamberlain area on the Missouri River was called 'the most dangerous and difficult' part of the entire Corps of Discovery journey by Captain William Clark in 1804 — the current, the weather, and the riverbed made it the only part that nearly stopped them.
days.culture: The Great Plains are the least cinematically represented American landscape — they appear in film as blank emptiness to be passed through. But as a rider, the plains are an extended meditation. The rhythm of the engine, the flat line of the horizon, the absence of visual distraction — it creates a mental clarity that mountain and coast riding does not. This episode should reflect that honestly.
talkingPoints: The mental game of long flat days: what your brain does with 600 km of nothing
The Missouri River's historical weight: the Lewis & Clark spine of continental exploration
Mayo Clinic: how one tornado in 1863 created the world's greatest medical institution
Minnesota's 10,000 lakes: the last major geological gift of the retreating glaciers
days.cta: What's the longest single riding or driving day you've ever done? Distance and hours — tell me everything.

days.videoTitle: Wisconsin's Cheese, Beer, and the Driftless Zone | Day 33
days.hook: One part of the Midwest was never touched by glaciers. The rest was flattened. You're about to ride through both in one day.
days.storyArc: Rochester south to Wisconsin. The Driftless Area: an oddly hilly, almost European landscape that escaped glaciation. Wisconsin dairy farms — the scale of the cheese industry here. Oconomowoc: a lakeside town with a Potawatomi name nobody spells correctly. The Great Lakes beginning to feel close.
shots: 'Welcome to Wisconsin — America's Dairyland' sign
Driftless Area: rolling hills, coulees, narrow valleys — unexpected for the Midwest
A Wisconsin dairy farm: silos, Holsteins, the pastoral reality
Oconomowoc main street: small town lakeside Wisconsin
Lake Oconomowoc or Lac La Belle: evening light on a Midwest lake
facts: Wisconsin produces approximately 25% of all cheese made in the United States — more than 600 varieties of cheese are produced here. Wisconsin cheese makers have won more gold medals at international competitions than any other US state.

The Driftless Area of southwest Wisconsin (and parts of Minnesota, Iowa, and Illinois) was never covered by the last glaciation — the ice sheets split around it, creating an island of pre-glacial topography with deep valleys, caves, and cold-water streams that were preserved when surrounding land was flattened.
Oconomowoc is a Potawatomi word meaning 'waterfall in the river' — it is one of the most commonly misspelled place names in Wisconsin.

Milwaukee, Wisconsin was the undisputed beer capital of America in the 19th and early 20th centuries — Miller, Pabst, Schlitz, and Blatz were all headquartered there. The phrase 'Milwaukee's Best' predates the beer brand by decades.

The Wisconsin Idea — established in 1904 — is the principle that the University of Wisconsin should extend its influence beyond campus to improve the state. It became the model for how American public universities were integrated into state governance and policy.
days.culture: Wisconsin is part of a Midwest identity that is often reduced to jokes about cheese and cold. The reality is a sophisticated food culture, a strong labor history (the state essentially invented the American labor union movement), and an outdoors culture built around the Great Lakes and the Mississippi tributaries.
talkingPoints: The Driftless Area: the geological accident that created Wisconsin's most interesting landscape
Why Wisconsin cheese is genuinely world-class — not just quantity, real quality
Oconomowoc name: 15 attempts to spell it correctly
Tomorrow: Lake Michigan and the Third Coast
days.cta: What's your favorite cheese and where did you first eat something that changed how you thought about food?

days.videoTitle: The Third Coast, the Border, and the Last Night Before Home | Day 34
days.hook: Lake Michigan is 1,640 miles of shoreline that nobody talks about. Then, after lunch with a host in the fruit belt, I cross back into Canada — for the first time in 34 days.
days.storyArc: Oconomowoc east toward Lake Michigan — the Third Coast revealing itself: sand dunes, orchards, lighthouses, fresh water the color of the Caribbean. Stop in Benton Harbor with John Cody Smith for lunch and filming — the economic and social story of this divided twin city. Then north and east to Port Huron, and the Blue Water Bridge: Canada. The CBSA crossing — coming home is a different experience than going in. Windsor/Sarnia to London, Ontario: the final overnight before Toronto. The trip is almost over, and you can feel it.
shots: Lake Michigan from the highway: the first sight of the inland sea — it genuinely looks oceanic
Indiana Dunes (if routing through) — climbing the dune with Lake Michigan below
St. Joseph/Benton Harbor shoreline: lighthouse, fresh water, blue horizon
Benton Harbor stop with John Cody Smith: the human moment — conversation, food, the host culture
Michigan fruit belt: roadside peach stands, blueberry farms, cherry orchards
Port Huron/Blue Water Bridge approach — two countries separated by a channel of water
CBSA booth: coming home — 'where have you been?' '34 days in America.'
'Welcome to Ontario' sign — the reaction shot
London, Ontario evening: reflective, quiet, final night before home
facts: Lake Michigan is 307 miles long, 118 miles wide, and 925 feet deep — it contains 1,180 cubic miles of fresh water and is visible from space. It is the only Great Lake entirely within the United States.
The Indiana Dunes include Mount Baldy, a dune 126 feet tall that migrates approximately 4 feet inland every year as wind moves it away from Lake Michigan.

Michigan produces more varieties of fruit than any other continental US state — this is directly caused by Lake Michigan moderating temperatures in the 'fruit belt' along its eastern shore. Michigan produces ~75% of all US tart cherries.

The Whirlpool Corporation, the world's largest home appliance manufacturer, is headquartered in Benton Harbor — in a city with one of the highest poverty rates in Michigan. St. Joseph, one mile away, has a median household income more than four times higher.

The Blue Water Bridge (Port Huron–Sarnia) consists of two parallel spans — the original 1938 suspension bridge and the 1997 cable-stayed addition. Together they handle over 8 million vehicle crossings per year.

Windsor, Ontario is at approximately 42°18'N — geographically south of Chicago, Cleveland, and Portland, Oregon. It is the southernmost point in Canada and the only place where you drive south to enter the United States from Canada.
London, Ontario was seriously considered as the capital of the Province of Canada in the 1840s — it lost to Ottawa primarily for reasons of political balance between French and English Canada.
days.culture: The Windsor-Detroit crossing has a specific cultural weight for Canadians — it is the most economically important land border crossing in North America, handling $250 million USD in trade per day. The Blue Water Bridge crossing at Port Huron/Sarnia is quieter and often faster on holiday weekends. Crossing back into Canada after 34 days in the US produces a specific re-orientation: the road signs change, the measurement system changes, the flag changes, and you realize how much of the last month you spent adapting to a country that is almost — but not quite — home.
talkingPoints: The Great Lakes as the world's largest fresh water system — the most strategically important geography in North America
Benton Harbor vs. St. Joseph: two cities separated by a mile and decades of structural inequality
The fruit belt microclimate: how a lake creates a world-class agricultural zone
Crossing back into Canada: what 34 days in America did to your sense of home
London, Ontario: the city that might have been Canada's capital
Jul 4 afternoon border crossing: why the traffic is lighter going into Canada today than tomorrow
days.cta: If you've crossed the Canada-US border by motorcycle — tell me your crossing story. Best or worst experience. I want to know.

days.videoTitle: 35 Days Across America — Home. What I Learned. What Changed. | Day 35 (FINALE)
days.hook: 35 days ago, I left this driveway. Today, I come back. Same bike. Different person.
days.storyArc: The finale. London to Toronto on the 401. Don't rush this — arrive slowly and deliberately. The city revealing itself: the skyline, the CN Tower, the familiar streets. Helmet off. Engine off. The ritual of arriving. Then: the reflection video. This is your season finale — the emotional capstone of the entire series.
shots: London departure: last morning on the road, early light
Highway 401: the busiest highway in North America — what it feels like to be home on the most Canadian road
Toronto skyline appearing over the horizon — slow approach
Gardiner Expressway: the final approach — recognize every exit
Final parking: driveway, garage, the same spot you left
Helmet on the handlebar: the universal motorcycle journey closing shot
facts: Highway 401 through Toronto is the busiest highway in North America by traffic volume — at its peak downtown section, it carries approximately 500,000 vehicles per day.
Toronto is the most ethnically diverse city in the world — over 51% of its residents were born outside Canada. More than 200 languages are spoken within city limits.
The CN Tower was the world's tallest free-standing structure from 1976 until 2007 when the Burj Khalifa surpassed it.
Toronto's Indigenous name is from the Mohawk language — 'Tkaronto' means 'where there are trees standing in the water,' referring to the wooden stakes used in fish weirs near the lake.

The Canadian motorcycle industry has produced some of the most important riders in history: Mike Hailwood (British-born but Canadian career), Steve Baker, and the entire adventure touring culture that grew out of the Canada-US cross-border riding tradition.
days.culture: Coming home from a long solo ride is a specific kind of transition. The world that seemed large is suddenly compressed back into the familiar. The things that felt permanent — the routine, the house, the neighborhood — have the strange quality of being both exactly the same and completely changed. For a YouTube audience that has watched 35 episodes, this moment is what they've been building toward. Give it the time it deserves.
talkingPoints: The 35-day recap: the three chapters (East, West, Home) and what each taught you
What the DR650 taught you — the bike that made all of this possible
What you'd do differently: the honest answer
A First Nations land acknowledgment for ending a journey through Indigenous North America
What's next: tease the next adventure or next season
days.cta: If you watched every episode — tell me the Day number that hit hardest for you. I want to read every one of these.
